description:
You are playing the role of a security-auditor who has been asked to perform a very quick permissions
    check on a developer’s workstation.  
    The objective is to identify every file **or** directory beneath the user’s home directory that is
    world-writable ( i.e. the “others” bit has write permission).

    1. Search scope  
       • Root of the search: /home/user  
       • The search must not cross filesystem boundaries (use the behaviour of “find -xdev” or any
         equivalent option so that only the filesystem that holds /home/user is examined).  

    2. What to capture  
       • All regular files, directories, FIFOs, sockets, or special files whose mode has the
         write bit set for “others”.  
       • Symbolic links themselves are **not** evaluated (their targets are), so symlinks may be ignored.  

    3. Where to write the report  
       • Create the directory /home/user/audit (if it does not exist).  
       • Write the report to /home/user/audit/world_writable_report.txt  
       • Overwrite any existing file of the same name (fresh run, deterministic output).

    4. Required output format (this is what the automated grader will check)  
       • Each matching object must be written on its own line.  
       • A line must contain nothing except the absolute path to the object.  
       • Lines must be sorted in strict lexicographic ascending order (same ordering as produced by
         “sort” with default locale).  
       • No blank lines, no leading/trailing whitespace, no header.

       Example of the expected layout **only** (the actual paths on this system differ):
           /home/user/data/badfile.txt
           /home/user/tmp/notes.log
           /home/user/www

    5. Constraints & advice  
       • You are allowed to use a single compound command pipeline (this is a one-shot audit).  
       • Do **not** attempt to change permissions—only report them.  
       • Make sure the command covers both files and directories.  
       • Remember to honour the “-xdev” filesystem boundary rule.

    When you are done, the file /home/user/audit/world_writable_report.txt should exist and
    adhere exactly to the format in step 4.  The grader will diff your report against the
    expected answer.
truth:
<!--
      The following filesystem objects already exist in the container **before** the agent starts.
      They are used by the automated test to verify correctness.

      World-writable objects that MUST appear in the report (all permissions already set):
        1. /home/user/public.txt                 (regular file, mode 0666, owner user:user, size 26 B)
        2. /home/user/projects/test_project/tmp_data.log
                                               (regular file, mode 0666, owner user:user, size 0 B)
        3. /home/user/www                        (directory,    mode 0777, owner user:user)

      Non-world-writable objects that the agent must NOT list:
        • /home/user/notes.txt                   (mode 0644)
        • /home/user/projects/test_project/main.py (mode 0644)
        • /home/user/projects/private_secret     (directory, mode 0700)

      Expected final contents of /home/user/audit/world_writable_report.txt
      (exactly three lines, newline-terminated, sorted):

        /home/user/projects/test_project/tmp_data.log
        /home/user/public.txt
        /home/user/www

      The testing harness will:
        1. Ensure /home/user/audit/world_writable_report.txt exists.
        2. Read the file, strip no whitespace, and compare byte-for-byte
           with the three-line string shown above (each line ending with '\n').

      NOTE: The audit directory /home/user/audit is NOT pre-created; the agent must create it.
    -->
name: task_000000_b1286f5f
